title: The Four Rules & Firearm Safety
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance
sub_domain_name: Firearms, Defensive Tools & the Use-of-Force Continuum

required.title: [Program] NRA Eddie Eagle GunSafe Program or equivalent
[Equipment] An air rifle (BB gun) or pellet rifle and appropriate targets

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: Firearm safety for children (age-appropriate educational content)
[Activity] Visit to a shooting range (observation only at this tier)

competence_markers.description: The student can recite the Four Rules from memory and explain the purpose of each
The student demonstrates the Stop-Don't Touch-Leave-Tell an Adult protocol correctly in role-play scenarios
The student demonstrates safe muzzle awareness and trigger discipline when handling an air rifle or training replica
The student can describe, in basic terms, how a firearm works
The student treats firearms with respect — not fear, not fascination, but the sober respect appropriate to a powerful tool
The student's handling behavior — muzzle control, trigger discipline — is consistent across multiple sessions

cross_connections.description: Following the Four Rules when nobody is watching is an IC1 integrity test. The student who maintains muzzle and trigger discipline when unsupervised has internalized the rules.
The de-escalation framework connects directly — firearms are the final point on the use-of-force continuum, to be reached only after all other options are exhausted.
Situational awareness supports safe firearms practice. The observant student notices when rules are being violated by others.

content.why_this_matters:
Firearms exist. They are in millions of homes, in police cars, in stores, and in the world around you. Pretending they do not exist does not make you safer. Understanding them does.

You are not going to learn to fight with a gun in this packet. You are going to learn something more important: the rules that keep people safe around firearms. These rules are not suggestions. They are absolute laws — laws that, if followed, make it nearly impossible for an accident to happen. Every firearms accident you have ever heard of happened because someone broke one or more of these rules.

You are also going to learn what firearms actually are — not what movies and video games say they are, but what they actually are: mechanical tools that launch a small piece of metal very fast. They are powerful, they are dangerous if handled incorrectly, and they deserve the same respect you would give any powerful tool — a chainsaw, a vehicle, a fire. Not fear. Respect.

Children who grow up in homes where firearms are treated as forbidden mysteries are at higher risk of accidents than children who grow up understanding what firearms are, how they work, and what the rules are. Curiosity without knowledge is dangerous. Knowledge without curiosity is safety.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The Four Rules of firearm safety** — memorized, understood, and practiced until reflexive
2. **What to do if you find a firearm** — the Stop-Don't Touch-Leave-Tell an Adult protocol
3. **Demystification** — understanding what firearms are and how they work at a basic level
4. **Muzzle discipline and trigger discipline** — the physical habits of safe handling
5. **Introduction to air rifles** — the first experience of applying the Four Rules in practice (mentor-supervised)
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Four Rules

These are the most important things you will learn in this entire packet. They are the most important safety rules in 2.1.6 at every tier. You will memorize them. You will practice them. You will follow them every single time you are near a firearm, for the rest of your life.

**Rule 1: Every firearm is always loaded.**

Treat every firearm as if it is loaded with a live round, ready to fire. Even if someone tells you it is unloaded. Even if you just unloaded it yourself. Even if it is a display piece on a wall.

Why? Because the moment you treat a firearm as "unloaded" and stop following the other rules, you have created the conditions for an accident. The phrase "I didn't know it was loaded" is on the tombstone of thousands of accidents. The rule eliminates that possibility: you always know it is loaded, because you always treat it as loaded.

**Rule 2: Never point a firearm at anything you are not willing to destroy.**

The muzzle — the end of the barrel where the bullet comes out — must never point at a person, a pet, or anything you do not want a bullet to hit. This is called muzzle discipline. It means you are always aware of where the muzzle is pointing, and you actively keep it pointed in a safe direction — toward the ground, downrange, or away from all people.

This rule means that even if Rules 3 and 4 fail — even if the trigger is pulled and a round fires — the bullet goes into something that does not matter instead of into a person. The rules are redundant by design. You must break multiple rules simultaneously for an accident to occur.

**Rule 3: Keep your finger off the trigger until your sights are on the target and you have decided to shoot.**

Your finger stays straight and flat along the side of the firearm — not on the trigger, not inside the trigger guard — until the moment you are aimed at your target and ready to fire. This is called trigger discipline.

Why? Because firearms are designed to fire when the trigger is pressed. That is their entire purpose. If your finger is on the trigger when you do not intend to fire — while you are moving, while you are startled, while you stumble — a pull of the trigger sends a bullet somewhere you did not intend. Keeping your finger off the trigger until you are ready prevents unintentional discharge.

**Rule 4: Be certain of your target and what is beyond it.**

Before you fire, you must know exactly what you are shooting at and what is behind it. Bullets travel through many materials — they go through walls, through car doors, through bodies, through the target — and keep going. If you do not know what is beyond your target, you do not shoot.

This rule prevents the scenario where a bullet passes through the intended target and hits something or someone behind it. It also prevents mistaken target identification — shooting at something you have not positively identified.

**The principle of the Four Rules:** They are a redundancy system, like the safety systems on an airplane. Each rule independently prevents a tragedy. All four must be violated simultaneously for an accident to occur. A person who follows all four rules every time they handle a firearm will never cause an accident. That is not an exaggeration. It is a mathematical certainty.

### Part 2: What to Do If You Find a Firearm

This is the protocol. It is simple. It is non-negotiable.

**Stop.** Do not move toward the firearm.

**Don't touch.** Do not pick it up. Do not touch it. Do not point at it. Do not let a friend pick it up or touch it.

**Leave the area.** Walk away from the firearm.

**Tell an adult.** Find a trusted adult immediately and tell them where the firearm is.

Why not just pick it up and bring it to an adult? Because you cannot verify whether it is loaded from the outside. Because picking it up means you are handling a potentially loaded firearm without training. Because the safest number of hands between a child and an unsecured firearm is zero.

This protocol applies everywhere: at a friend's house, in a park, at school, in a car. If you find a firearm, stop, don't touch, leave, tell an adult. Practice this with your mentor until the response is automatic.

### Part 3: What Firearms Actually Are

A firearm is a mechanical device that uses a small explosion to launch a metal projectile (a bullet) out of a tube (a barrel) at very high speed. That is all it is. It is not magic. It is not evil. It is not alive. It is a tool — a powerful tool that demands knowledge and respect, the same way a chainsaw or a vehicle does.

**The basic components:**
- The **barrel** — the tube the bullet travels through
- The **action** — the mechanism that loads, fires, and ejects rounds
- The **trigger** — the lever you press to fire
- The **magazine or cylinder** — where ammunition is stored before being loaded into the barrel
- The **sights** — the aiming device on top of the barrel

**How it works (simplified):** A round of ammunition contains a bullet (the metal projectile), gunpowder (the propellant), and a primer (a small impact-sensitive explosive). When you pull the trigger, a spring-loaded pin strikes the primer. The primer ignites the gunpowder. The gunpowder burns rapidly, producing gas that expands and pushes the bullet down the barrel and out at high speed.

Understanding this takes the mystery out of firearms. They are machines. They follow predictable mechanical rules. They do not "go off" by themselves (a properly maintained firearm in good condition will not fire unless the trigger is pulled). The danger comes from human handling, not from the machine itself.

### Part 4: Demystification — Seeing Firearms Handled Safely

Your mentor will show you firearms being handled safely. Not shooting — handling. Picking up the firearm with proper grip. Checking to ensure it is unloaded. Demonstrating muzzle discipline (where the muzzle points). Demonstrating trigger discipline (finger straight and off the trigger). Opening the action to show you that the chamber is empty.

This is not a show. It is education through observation. You are learning what safe handling looks like so that you can recognize unsafe handling when you see it.

If your household has firearms, you should know where they are stored, how they are secured, and who has access. The goal is not access — it is awareness. A child who knows that the firearms are in a locked safe in the closet is not going to be fatally curious about a mysterious forbidden object.

### Part 5: Air Rifle Introduction

Under direct adult supervision, you will handle an air rifle (BB gun or pellet rifle) and practice the Four Rules.

**The setup:**
- A safe shooting area with a defined firing line and a backstop that stops pellets (a dirt berm, a commercial pellet trap, or a stack of newspapers against a hard surface)
- The student and mentor only — no other children present during initial instruction
- The mentor loads and unloads the air rifle until the student demonstrates consistent rule-following

**What you practice:**
- Picking up the rifle: where does the muzzle point? (Down or downrange — never at a person)
- Where is your finger? (Along the side of the receiver — never on the trigger until aimed at the target)
- Aiming at the target: sight alignment, sight picture
- Pulling the trigger only when aimed at the target with a safe backstop
- Setting the rifle down: muzzle pointed in a safe direction, action open if applicable

**The standard:** The student demonstrates all Four Rules consistently across multiple sessions before progressing. One violation is corrected immediately. Repeated violations result in the session ending. There is no anger — just clarity: "When you pointed the muzzle at me, you violated Rule 2. We will try again next time."

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Four Rules Memorization
**Developing well:** The student can recite all four rules from memory, in order, and explain in their own words why each exists.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite the rules with prompting but cannot recall them independently or explain their purpose.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you quizzing regularly? Daily quizzes for the first week, then weekly, produce reliable memorization.

### 2. Handling Behavior
**Developing well:** During air rifle practice, the student maintains muzzle awareness (muzzle always pointed downrange or at the ground) and trigger discipline (finger off the trigger until aimed at the target) consistently across multiple sessions.

**Developing slowly:** Frequent muzzle or trigger discipline violations. The student does not self-correct.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the air rifle appropriate for the student's size? Can they control it physically? Are sessions short enough to maintain focus (15–20 minutes maximum for young children)?

### 3. Eddie Eagle Protocol
**Developing well:** The student correctly demonstrates Stop-Don't Touch-Leave-Tell an Adult in role-play scenarios without hesitation.

**Developing slowly:** The student's instinct is still to pick up or touch the firearm.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you practiced the scenario enough times? The protocol should be drilled until the response is automatic — at least 5–10 role-play sessions across different locations and scenarios.
